title: CI vs CD vs Continuous Deployment
description:
## Three Terms That Get Confused

Three teams describe their pipelines:

- **Team 1**: "Every merge to main automatically builds the app and runs the full test suite."
- **Team 2**: "Every change that passes our pipeline is always kept ready to ship, but a human clicks the button to push it to production."
- **Team 3**: "Every change that passes our pipeline goes straight to production automatically — no one approves it."

Which statement correctly distinguishes **Continuous Integration**, **Continuous Delivery**, and **Continuous Deployment**?

options.text: Team 1 is Continuous Integration (automated build + test on merge), Team 2 is Continuous Delivery (always releasable, final push is a manual decision), and Team 3 is Continuous Deployment (every passing change auto-releases to production).
Team 1 is Continuous Deployment, Team 2 is Continuous Integration, and Team 3 is Continuous Delivery.
All three describe Continuous Integration; the terms Delivery and Deployment are just marketing synonyms for it.
Team 2 is Continuous Deployment because it deploys, and Team 3 is Continuous Delivery because a human is never involved.

explanation: These three terms describe successive stages of automation. **Continuous Integration (CI)** is the foundation: every change merged is automatically **built and tested** so integration problems surface early (Team 1). **Continuous Delivery** builds on CI by keeping every passing change **always in a releasable state**, but the actual promotion to production is a deliberate **manual (one-click) decision** (Team 2). **Continuous Deployment** removes that final human gate — **every change that passes the pipeline is automatically released to production** with no manual approval (Team 3). The key discriminator between the two CDs is the production gate: Delivery = automatic up to a manual go/no-go; Deployment = fully automatic all the way to prod. Option b scrambles the mapping. Option c is wrong — they are distinct concepts, not synonyms. Option d inverts Delivery and Deployment.

hints.title: Focus on the production gate
hints.body: All three automate building and testing. The thing that separates Continuous Delivery from Continuous Deployment is one question: does a human have to approve the release to production?
